title: The Highway of Tears — Canada's Ignored Crisis
description: Along a 724-kilometer stretch of Highway 16 in British Columbia, at least 18 women and girls — most of them Indigenous — have gone missing or been murdered since the 1970s. For decades, Canadian authorities provided inadequate resources and attention to these cases. The Highway of Tears became a symbol of a national crisis in the treatment of Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women and Girls.

segments.voice: Highway 16. The Yellowhead Highway. Seven hundred and twenty-four kilometers of asphalt running east to west across the interior of British Columbia, through forests, past rivers, through towns too small to sustain a bus service and too remote for reliable cell coverage. For decades, young women — most of them Indigenous — walked the highway's shoulder and disappeared. Their names took years to enter the public consciousness. Some never did.
segments.visual: British Columbia Highway 16 remote forest mountains road

segments.voice: The cases stretch back to 1969. Fifteen-year-old Gloria Moody. In 1970, Micheline Pare. In 1971, Gale Weys. Through the 1970s, 80s, and 90s, women went missing from communities along the highway. Some bodies were found in the forest. Some were found along the roadside. Some were never found at all. The families grieved. The investigations were brief, underfunded, and produced almost no results.

The term 'Highway of Tears' is attributed to Indigenous communities in the region who began using it to name what they could see was a pattern — a concentration of violence against women along this specific route. The name expressed grief, frustration, and a demand for recognition. It would take decades before that recognition came from Canadian law enforcement and government.
segments.visual: Indigenous community grief naming recognition frustration

segments.voice: Why did women walk alone on this highway? Because many of the communities along Highway 16 had no affordable public transportation. No Greyhound service to smaller stops. No taxis. Young women who needed to travel between communities for work, for school, for family — had limited options. Hitchhiking was dangerous. But for many, it was the only option. The absence of infrastructure created the conditions for predation.
segments.visual: no bus service remote community transportation gap poverty

segments.voice: The racial dimension of the Highway of Tears is not subtle. The vast majority of the missing and murdered women were Indigenous. Studies of media coverage from the 1970s through the 1990s show minimal attention to these cases compared to comparable cases involving white women. Police resources allocated to the cases were demonstrably less. The systemic devaluation of Indigenous women's lives was visible in every aspect of the response.
segments.visual: media coverage disparity Indigenous women race comparison

segments.voice: In 2006, the RCMP — the Royal Canadian Mounted Police — announced a formal investigation called Project E-PANA, dedicated to unsolved highway murders and disappearances in British Columbia. By 2009, Project E-PANA was examining 18 cases specifically along Highway 16, as well as dozens more across British Columbia. The creation of this task force was an acknowledgment — however belated — that these cases had not received adequate attention.
segments.visual: RCMP Project E-PANA task force 2006 British Columbia

segments.voice: One man has been linked to multiple Highway of Tears cases: Bobby Jack Fowler, an American drifter who worked construction jobs in British Columbia in the 1970s. DNA evidence connected Fowler to the 1974 murder of Colleen MacMillen, 16, whose body was found near Hixon. Fowler died in an Oregon prison in 2006, serving time for an unrelated sexual assault. He could not be prosecuted for the BC murders after his death.
segments.visual: Bobby Jack Fowler DNA Colleen MacMillen drifter 1970s

segments.voice: A second confirmed case: Cody Alan Legebokoff, a 19-year-old hockey player and small-town Canadian boy who was convicted in 2010 of four murders in the Fort St. John area — north of Highway 16. He was charming, well-liked, and looked nothing like the public image of a killer. He had killed four women in 2009. His case added to the mounting evidence that the violence along BC's northern highways had multiple perpetrators.
segments.visual: Cody Legebokoff hockey player convicted four murders 2010

segments.voice: The 2019 National Inquiry into Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women and Girls in Canada produced a landmark report. After two years of hearings, the inquiry concluded that what had happened to Indigenous women across Canada — including along the Highway of Tears — constituted genocide under international law. This was not a metaphorical conclusion. The panel used the legal definition and applied it with documented evidence.
segments.visual: national inquiry MMIWG genocide report 2019 Canada

segments.voice: The inquiry's use of the word genocide was deeply controversial. Some embraced it as the only accurate term for the systematic devaluation and destruction of Indigenous women's lives. Others, including some politicians, pushed back against the legal framing. The debate about the word obscured, for a time, the report's 231 Calls for Justice — concrete recommendations for change in policing, law, education, and social services.
segments.visual: genocide debate calls for justice 231 recommendations

segments.voice: The Calls for Justice have been partially implemented. Some police forces have created dedicated MMIWG units. Some provinces have improved transportation infrastructure in remote communities. The federal government created a national action plan in 2021. Advocates and families of victims have consistently argued that implementation has been slow and incomplete.
segments.visual: implementation slow national action plan 2021 incomplete

segments.voice: The families of women taken along the Highway of Tears have carried their grief with extraordinary public dignity. Matilda Wilson, whose daughter Ramona was murdered in 1994, became one of the most prominent advocates for the investigation. Gladys Radek, whose niece Tamara Chipman disappeared in 2005, co-founded Walk4Justice, a cross-Canada march for awareness. These are the women who forced the country to see.
segments.visual: family advocates Matilda Wilson Gladys Radek Walk4Justice

segments.voice: Tamara Chipman disappeared in September 2005 at the age of 22. She had been hitchhiking on Highway 16. Her body has never been found. Her aunt Gladys Radek has spent nearly 20 years searching for Tamara and advocating for all the other women the highway has taken. She has said that Tamara's disappearance 'changed everything' for her — that she could not be silent in the face of what the highway had taken.
segments.visual: Tamara Chipman missing 2005 Gladys Radek advocacy

segments.voice: The communities along Highway 16 — Prince Rupert, Smithers, Burns Lake, Vanderhoof, Prince George — are real places with real histories and real people. They are not simply waypoints in a crime narrative. The violence that occurred along this road did not happen in abstraction. It happened to women with names and families in communities with histories that go back thousands of years before the highway was paved.
segments.visual: Highway 16 communities real places Indigenous history

segments.voice: Multiple active investigations remain open along Highway 16. Project E-PANA continues. New forensic techniques are being applied to older evidence. Cold case units are interviewing new witnesses as communities become more willing to speak to investigators. The DNA of suspects recovered in some cases has not yet been matched to any known individual in databases — but databases grow.
segments.visual: active investigations cold case forensic new technology

segments.voice: The Highway of Tears is not a true crime story in the traditional sense. It is a pattern of violence shaped by colonialism, poverty, racism, inadequate infrastructure, and institutional indifference. It is also a story of survival — of families and communities who refused to allow their women to be forgotten, who named the highway, who walked its length, who testified before national inquiries, who kept saying: they were here, they mattered, and the world had a responsibility to respond.
segments.visual: colonialism poverty institutional failure survival community
